tools: AWS IAM Roles for Service Accounts / IRSA
rows.evidence: IAM policy export showing zero static long-lived credentials in the path. OIDC trust policy showing federated identity provider configured. Rotation logs from any remaining service accounts (target: none in 12 months).
rows.notes: Often confused with credential rotation — rotation alone doesn't close the audit finding. The control requires federated identity (the workload proves who it is to the cloud), not just shorter-lived static creds. Foundation for SOCI 2022 SLACIP-amendment maturity.

rows.evidence: Static + runtime analysis report: count of wildcard (*) actions in production policies, trending to zero. AccessAnalyzer findings cleared monthly.
rows.notes: Wildcards in production IAM are the most common audit finding. The control is *demonstrated* least-privilege (Access Analyzer says so), not *intended* least-privilege.

tools: AWS IAM Identity Center + permission sets
rows.evidence: Just-in-time access requests log. Approval audit trail. Session recording for production breakglass. Account-of-last-resort tested quarterly.
rows.notes: E8 ML2+ requires PAM, not 'we have admins with persistent access we trust.'

rows.ctrl: MFA enforced for all human access (no exceptions)
rows.evidence: Conditional access policy export. Zero users with MFA-disabled status. WebAuthn / passkey adoption % trending up; SMS/voice OTP trending to zero.
rows.notes: SMS-OTP no longer counts as strong MFA per NIST SP 800-63B Revision 4 draft. Passkeys / hardware tokens are the target.

rows.evidence: Signed in-toto / SLSA provenance attached to every production artefact. Rekor transparency log entry for every signed artefact. Verification step in deploy pipeline that *blocks* if signature absent or invalid.
rows.notes: SLSA L2 = signed provenance; L3 = hardened builder (isolated, ephemeral). L3 needed for US-federal procurement under CISA SSA in many cases.

rows.evidence: SBOM per artefact stored centrally. CISA KEV catalogue subscribed; on match, owner-of-service alerted within minutes (not central security inbox). MTTR for KEV-listed CVEs measured and trending down.
rows.notes: Emitting SBOMs is necessary but not sufficient. The audit-passing control is the *loop*: SBOM → vulnerability → owner-alert → patch within SLA. Pure SBOM-collection = shelfware.

rows.evidence: Admission-controller logs showing rejection of unsigned/untrusted images. Policy-as-code repo defining trusted signers + repositories. Test workload with bad signature must fail.
rows.notes: Verifying signatures at deploy-time, not at scan-time. Catches the malicious-rebuild and tampered-mirror attack class.

rows.evidence: Same source produces byte-identical artefact across builds. Diffoscope output showing no drift. CI configured to reject non-hermetic dependencies.
rows.notes: Hard to reach for most teams; necessary for the most exposed federal / defence workloads (SLSA L4).

rows.ctrl: Locked dependency versions (no floating tags / ranges in prod)
rows.evidence: Lockfile present + committed for every project. CI rejects builds whose lockfile is older than X weeks or where dependencies drift from lock.
rows.notes: Floating tags (:latest, ^1.x) make supply chain unsafe at any speed. Lockfile + freshness SLA is the control.

rows.evidence: Column-level lineage graph from source-of-truth to downstream consumers. BCBS 239 risk-aggregation reports traceable to source systems with timestamp.
rows.notes: BCBS 239 has been live since 2016 and is still failed by most G-SIBs at audit. Lineage at column level (not table level) is the bar.

rows.ctrl: Data subject access / deletion (DSAR) within statutory window
tools: Custom RPC + queue per data domain
rows.evidence: Time-bounded DSAR pipeline. Tested quarterly. Per-data-domain owners with documented response SLAs. Audit log of every DSAR fulfilled.
rows.notes: Statutory windows: GDPR 30 days, AU Privacy Act 30 days (under reform), HIPAA 30 days for access requests. Untested DSAR pipelines fail at first regulator-driven test.

rows.evidence: Every data asset tagged. Handling rules (encryption, retention, residency) inherited from classification. DLP rules tested via canary records.
rows.notes: The control is not 'we have a data classification standard'. It's 'every dataset wears its classification and the platform enforces handling rules.'

rows.evidence: Tenant-to-region routing table. Network policies that block egress to other regions. Sample request trace showing data stays in-region end-to-end.
rows.notes: Soft-residency (claim) vs hard-residency (architecturally enforceable). Auditors increasingly want the latter.

rows.ctrl: Eval-set gating in CI for AI applications
rows.evidence: Versioned eval set per prompt + model + version. CI run on every change that touches prompt or model. Merge blocked on critical-regression. Eval-set drift report quarterly.
rows.notes: EU AI Act Art. 15 (accuracy / robustness) is enforceable only with eval-set discipline. NIST AI RMF MEASURE function depends on it.

rows.evidence: Guardrail policy version-controlled. Adversarial-prompt suite run weekly against guardrail (not against base model). False-negative + false-positive rate tracked. Per-incident postmortem when guardrail breached.
rows.notes: Guardrails encoded in the system prompt = no guardrail. The control is a distinct layer that intercepts before & after the model.

rows.evidence: Per request: prompt, retrieved context, model + version, guardrails applied, output, latency, cost. Signed, immutable, retention-policy-controlled. Replay-able at any time.
rows.notes: EU AI Act Art. 12 (logging) becomes operational evidence here. Without per-decision logs you cannot answer regulator's 'why did the model decide X for this customer'.

tools: Custom workflow engine + queue per domain
rows.evidence: Trigger conditions documented (confidence threshold, risk tier, regulator-defined). Human reviewer queue with SLA. Audit trail showing human approval per decision.
rows.notes: Art. 14 (human oversight) is operational, not declarative. 'A human can override' isn't oversight; 'a human must approve high-risk decisions' is.

rows.evidence: Quarterly red-team report with documented attack categories. Pre-deploy adversarial test suite. CVE-equivalent tracking for AI vulnerabilities found.
rows.notes: OWASP LLM01 (prompt injection) is the most common attack vector; defending requires both input-side and output-side controls.

rows.ctrl: Model card + data card for production model
rows.evidence: Per model in production: model card (training data, eval results, intended use, limitations). Per dataset: data card (provenance, consent basis, refresh cadence). Both versioned with the model.
rows.notes: EU AI Act technical documentation (Art. 11 / Annex IV) maps to model + data cards.

rows.evidence: Per critical service: RTO + RPO defined. Recovery tested at least annually with timed result. Gap-to-target tracked. Multi-region failover rehearsed.
rows.notes: CPS 230 (live in AU FS) requires this for every 'critical operation'. DORA RTS expects testing artefacts the regulator can review.

rows.evidence: Vendor catalogue with criticality classification. Concentration risk (% of critical operations on one vendor) measured. Exit-plan for top-N critical vendors documented.
rows.notes: DORA introduced critical ICT third-party designation (CTPP). APRA CPS 230 has equivalent material-service-provider rules. Both need real numbers, not 'we use AWS'.

rows.ctrl: Chaos engineering / failure injection in production
rows.evidence: Scheduled chaos experiments with documented blast radius. Hypothesis-driven (we expect X to happen; did it?). Postmortem when hypothesis was wrong.
rows.notes: DORA Art. 25 (threat-led penetration testing) implies dynamic resilience testing; chaos engineering is its operational analogue.

rows.evidence: Pre-templated regulator-notification drafts (per regulator). Drill quarterly: time from detection to draft sent. Audit trail of incident decisions.
rows.notes: US CIRCIA: 72h incident, 24h ransom payment. NIS2: 24h early-warning, 72h notification. SOCI 2018: critical infrastructure asset incidents 72h. The shape of the obligation is similar globally.

rows.ctrl: Blameless postmortems for every Sev-1 + Sev-2
rows.evidence: Postmortem published within 14 days of every Sev-1/2. Action items tracked with closure-SLA. Quarterly read-back: themes, contributing factors, systemic gaps.
rows.notes: Action items that don't close = postmortems that don't compound. Google SRE Workbook Chapter 10 is the canonical reference.

rows.ctrl: Patching SLA for KEV-listed CVEs (≤14 days)
rows.evidence: Per-CVE: detection time, patch-deployed time. MTTR for KEV-class trending below 14 days. Out-of-SLA list (in writing, with owner) for any gap.
rows.notes: CISA KEV is the actually-exploited subset; far better priority than CVSS-score alone.

rows.evidence: Per audit event: who, what, when, source. Retention per regulator (HIPAA 6 years; GDPR variable). Immutability proof (object-lock / write-once / hash-chain).
rows.notes: Local log files don't pass audit. Centralised + tamper-evident is the baseline.

rows.evidence: External scan (Qualys SSL Labs / testssl) showing A+ grade. Cipher allow-list documented. Audit log of any TLS-version-downgrade attempts.
rows.notes: PCI DSS 4.0 explicitly requires TLS 1.2+ (not 1.0/1.1) since 30 March 2025.

rows.ctrl: Per-customer / per-tenant KMS keys (BYOK or CMK)
rows.evidence: Per-tenant key inventory. Key-rotation cadence enforced. Key-deletion as a real operation (crypto-shredding for tenant offboarding).
rows.notes: BYOK only meaningful if you can demonstrate the key is exclusive to one tenant and rotated independently.

rows.evidence: Pre-commit + push-time + retroactive scanning. Detected-secret-to-revocation MTTR. Quarterly retroactive scan of all repos.
rows.notes: A leaked secret rotated in 30 days is a breach. Auto-revocation pipeline matters more than detection.

rows.evidence: Per service: deployment shape (canary % · linear N-min · blue-green). Auto-rollback metrics defined. Recent rollback events with timing.
rows.notes: Big-bang prod deploys are a CPS 230 finding-in-waiting. Progressive deployment with auto-rollback is the operational standard.

rows.ctrl: Application allowlisting (no unsigned code execution in prod)
rows.evidence: Endpoint policy export. Test execution of unsigned binary must be blocked. Exception list with owner + expiry.
rows.notes: E8 ML2+ requires application control. Hard to retrofit; bake into golden image / paved path.

rows.ctrl: Inherited golden signals (latency, traffic, errors, saturation)
rows.evidence: Per service: 4 golden signals dashboard inherited from paved path. SLO + error-budget per critical service. Alert quality (actionable-rate) ≥70%.
rows.notes: Inherited (paved path) beats per-team-set-up-their-own. The control is consistency across the fleet.

rows.evidence: Detection rules in git. PR-reviewed. CI-tested against historical events. Rule effectiveness reviewed monthly.
rows.notes: Detection-as-code beats SIEM-managed-by-vendor. Treats security like the engineering practice it is.

rows.ctrl: Network segmentation between IT and OT zones
tools: Palo Alto Networks NGFW with zones
rows.evidence: Zone-and-conduit model documented (per IEC 62443-3-2). Inter-zone traffic logged. Conduits monitored. Unauthorised flow alerts.
rows.notes: IEC 62443 zone-and-conduit model is now the de-facto bar for OT/ICS environments. SOCI 2022 SLACIP-amendment audits ask after this.

rows.evidence: 100% intra-cluster traffic encrypted (mesh dashboard). Strict mTLS mode (no plaintext fallback). Cert rotation cadence.
rows.notes: Service mesh is real platform investment. Adopt if 20+ services and a platform team; defer otherwise.

rows.ctrl: Production workload runs without privileged escalation
rows.evidence: Admission policy enforcing restricted PSS profile. No privileged: true in production. Exception list with owner + expiry.
rows.notes: Default-deny privileged execution; allow only with explicit exception. This is the bar Kubernetes Pod Security Standards 'restricted' enforces.

rows.evidence: Per quarter: every privileged user reviewed by a manager. Review evidence signed. Revocations logged with timing.
rows.notes: Manual UARs by spreadsheet collapse at scale. The control needs an attestation engine, not a Friday-afternoon Excel.

rows.ctrl: Critical operation register with substitutability analysis
rows.evidence: Critical operations enumerated. Per operation: substitute (manual / backup / contracted alternative) tested. Tolerance for disruption set.
rows.notes: CPS 230 explicitly requires this. The 'tolerance for disruption' is the regulator's pet metric.

rows.ctrl: Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with every PHI handler
rows.evidence: BAA registry. Every vendor processing PHI on signed BAA. Annual review. Termination + data-return clauses tested.
rows.notes: Major cloud providers offer pre-signed BAAs; smaller vendors often forgotten and become the audit finding.

rows.evidence: PMS plan document. Adverse-event capture mechanism. Periodic safety update report (PSUR) cadence. Field-action procedure tested.
rows.notes: FDA expects PMS for any clinical AI. Maps to ISO 14971 risk management.

rows.evidence: Every infra change via PR. Plan-time policy check blocks bad configs (public S3, root-account use, untagged resources). Out-of-band changes alerted.
rows.notes: ClickOps in production = audit finding. IaC + plan-time policy is the bar.

rows.ctrl: Drift detection (cloud config matches IaC source-of-truth)
rows.evidence: Daily / hourly drift report. Out-of-IaC changes alerted to owner. Closed-loop: drift → patched in code + applied, not patched in cloud alone.
rows.notes: Drift accumulates silently. Detection alone is necessary but not sufficient — the loop must close back to code.

rows.evidence: Per service / per tenant cost trend monthly. Cost spike (>30%) triggers owner alert. Cost-per-resolved-task / cost-per-transaction trending.
rows.notes: CPS 230 doesn't name cost explicitly, but financial sustainability of critical operations is in scope. FinOps Foundation Framework is the operational substrate.

rows.evidence: Crypto inventory (where is asymmetric crypto used). PQC transition plan with milestones. Pilot with hybrid (classical + PQC) on one critical path.
rows.notes: NIST finalised PQC standards Aug 2024 (FIPS 203/204/205). Regulators are starting to ask about transition plans.

rows.evidence: Per AI use-case: risk tier (per EU AI Act + internal taxonomy). Impact assessment signed. Review cadence + trigger events.
rows.notes: EU AI Act Art. 9 (risk management system). NIST AI RMF MAP function. ISO 42001 Annex A.6.1.

rows.evidence: All models scanned for serialised-code exploits. ML-BOM published per model. Training-data provenance documented.
rows.notes: Pickle files in shared model registries are an active attack surface. ML-supply-chain is the new software-supply-chain.

rows.evidence: Risk report → upstream system traceable. Adjustments logged. Materiality of each manual override classified.
rows.notes: G-SIBs were given BCBS 239 in 2013, due 2016, mostly still failing 2024 (BCBS reviews are public). The substrate is the gap, not the regulation.

rows.ctrl: Sovereign cloud region with assessed controls
rows.evidence: IRAP assessment letter from provider. Workload placement policy. Data egress controls preventing cross-region routing.
rows.notes: Up to PROTECTED on assessed regions; SECRET typically on sovereign-only platforms.

rows.ctrl: Immutable backups with offline / air-gapped copy
rows.evidence: Backup inventory + retention. Restore test quarterly. Immutability window > regulator-defined retention. Air-gap proven via network path test.
rows.notes: E8 ML2+ requires immutable + tested backups. Ransomware resilience is the underlying threat.

tools: Custom consent management with audit log
rows.evidence: Consent capture audit log per user. Revocation triggers downstream-system removal within N hours. Granularity matches actual purposes (not 'accept all').
rows.notes: Cookie banner alone doesn't satisfy. The control is propagation of revocation to every downstream consumer.

rows.evidence: VPN decommissioning roadmap (or done). Per-app access tied to identity + device posture. Audit log per access decision.
rows.notes: Always-on VPN is the legacy. ZTNA per-app, per-identity, per-posture is the bar.

rows.evidence: DNSSEC validated externally. CAA records limit cert issuance. SPF + DKIM + DMARC at p=reject. MTA-STS published.
rows.notes: Often skipped because 'we have HTTPS'; the DNS + email-auth controls close a different attack class.

rows.ctrl: API rate limiting + authentication on every endpoint
rows.evidence: Per endpoint: auth method documented (API key / OAuth / mTLS). Rate-limit profile applied. Unauthenticated endpoints registry (intentional exceptions only).
rows.notes: OWASP API Security Top 10 (BOLA, BOPLA, broken auth) maps to deficiencies here. API gateway + auth-per-endpoint is the structural control.

rows.ctrl: Automated OS patching (≤30 days for high-severity)
rows.evidence: Patch compliance dashboard. % of fleet patched within N days of release. Out-of-SLA list with mitigation.
rows.notes: E8 ML2 requires 48-hour patching for working exploits, 1-month for known vulns. The structural fix is immutable / auto-updating base images.

rows.ctrl: WAF with managed rule sets + bot management
rows.evidence: WAF enabled in blocking mode. Managed-rule subscription current. Custom rules version-controlled. Per-incident WAF-block review.
rows.notes: WAF in 'monitor' mode never moved to 'block' is the most common shelfware control.

rows.evidence: Quarterly board pack with technology / cyber / AI risk reporting. Action items tracked. Materiality threshold documented.
rows.notes: EU NIS2 explicit board accountability. APRA CPS 234 + CPS 230 board-level oversight. ISO 42001 top management responsibility.
